ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general hamburger entry (FOODON_00002427); no specific double-patty cheeseburger code exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW covers hamburger as a composite beef sandwich product; no distinct double-cheeseburger sub-code exists. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for double cheeseburger as a food product.

serving.description: 1 double cheeseburger (burger only, without fries or drink)
serving.natural_language_portion: One double cheeseburger (burger only, approximately 200 g) is a typical single-item serving, providing around 526 kcal, 26.6 g protein, 20.4 g fat, and 59.1 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Double Cheeseburger — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Foodo at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C were reported in the input at lower confidence (0.70) from a secondary platform source; these values are retained as best available but flagged. USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, item 21318 — Cheeseburger, double patty, plain) was cross-referenced to validate plausibility of macro and micronutrient values.

gi_source.title: Hamburger bun / refined wheat bread composite — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for double cheeseburger
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for a double cheeseburger as a composite dish. GI estimated at 66 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate vehicle — a refined wheat hamburger bun — which is documented in the University of Sydney GI Database at GI ~70 for white bread and ~61–66 for hamburger buns. The beef patties and cheese contribute negligible carbohydrate and lower the blended GI slightly. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (66) × carbohydrate per serving (59.1 g from 200 g portion) / 100 = 39.0, rounded to 38.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (487 mg/100 g) with low potassium (197 mg/100 g) and presence of trans fat and saturated fat
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid double cheeseburgers due to 487 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 974 mg per burger), a sodium-to-potassium ratio that is unfavourable for blood pressure control, and the presence of saturated fat (3.73 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) which contribute to arterial stiffness; if consumed, pair with water rather than a sugar-sweetened beverage and avoid additional condiments such as ketchup or pickles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (29.57 g/100 g) from a glazed white-flour bun with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g), yielding a medium-to-high glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat the double cheeseburger as an occasional food only, as the refined-flour glazed bun delivers approximately 59 g of carbohydrate per burger with only 3.6 g of fibre, producing a glycaemic load of approximately 39 per serving; consuming it as part of a set meal with fries and a sugar-sweetened beverage substantially amplifies postprandial glucose excursion and should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (3.73 g/100 g), trans fat (0.2 g/100 g), high sodium (487 mg/100 g), and cholesterol (29 mg/100 g) in a calorie-dense product (263 kcal/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of double cheeseburgers because each 200 g burger delivers approximately 7.5 g saturated fat and 0.4 g trans fat — both of which raise LDL cholesterol and promote atherosclerosis — alongside 974 mg sodium that elevates blood pressure; if consumed occasionally, choose a burger-only option without fries or sugar-sweetened beverages and account for the meal within daily saturated fat limits of less than 20 g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) and arachidonic acid from red beef patties amplify pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid double cheeseburgers because the beef patties are a concentrated source of arachidonic acid (omega-6) that directly amplifies joint inflammation, and the presence of trans fat at 0.2 g/100 g is a hard disqualifier for RA dietary management; deep-fried accompaniments such as French fries further generate advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that worsen synovial inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A Double Cheeseburger is a Western fast-food sandwich consisting of two grilled or pan-fried beef patties stacked in a soft glazed white-flour bun, layered with melted processed cheese slices and caramelised onions. The dish is calorie-dense at 263 kcal per 100 g, with a macronutrient profile of 13.3 g protein, 10.2 g fat, and 29.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g. The glazed bun is the dominant carbohydrate source and contributes to a medium glycaemic index of approximately 66, while the dual beef patties provide the majority of protein and saturated fat (3.73 g/100 g). Trans fat is present at 0.2 g/100 g from commercial beef and bun formulations. Sodium is elevated at 487 mg/100 g, making a single 200 g burger equivalent to approximately 974 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg. Calcium is notably present at 116 mg/100 g, contributed by the cheese. In Singapore, the double cheeseburger is widely available at fast-food chains and is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner item, often as part of a set meal with French fries and a soft drink. It is classified as halal in the Singapore context. Clinically, the double cheeseburger is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high sodium, refined carbohydrate load, saturated and trans fat content, and pro-inflammatory fatty acid profile. It is best treated as an occasional indulgence rather than a regular dietary component.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95 with exact match to the named dish. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C retained from secondary source at 0.70 confidence and flagged. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (item 21318) cross-referenced for plausibility validation. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for hamburger bun.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this specific double cheeseburger formulation is not reported in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy item 21318 at the composite dish level. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry for this dish; USDA SR Legacy item 21318 was consulted but values are for a generic plain double cheeseburger and were not mapped to avoid source mixing at differing confidence levels. Saturated fat (3.73 g/100 g), trans fat (0.2 g/100 g), and vitamin C (1.33 mg/100 g) were sourced from a secondary platform at confidence 0.70; these should be verified against a primary approved source in a future review cycle. The two portion entries in the input (750 g set meal and 650 g set meal) represent composite meal weights including fries and beverage; the serving size in this record is set to 200 g representing the burger patty-and-bun unit only, which is the nutritionally attributable portion for the double cheeseburger item. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database hamburger bun data; no direct GI study for a double cheeseburger composite was identified in the approved GI source.
